text:
Went here with my family, we really liked it. Me and my sister went to get ice cream and some dessert at a place and found out there more than 5, the attractions are pretty cool and fun.

We liked how walkable it is, it’s real impressive.

Food was great. We had a lot of fun and enjoyed every second of it

Navy Pier is such a nice area to shop, walk around, and just enjoy your time. There’s a little bit of everything—food, stores, views, and plenty of space to just take it all in. It’s a great spot to slow down and really experience the city atmosphere.

I also did the Centennial Wheel, and it was a good experience overall. The views are beautiful, especially seeing the skyline and the water from up high. It’s definitely one of those classic Chicago moments you have to try at least once.

I will say, it’s more family oriented, but it’s still an experience everyone should have when booking a trip to Chicago. It’s iconic, it’s fun, and it’s worth adding to your itinerary.

Great place for kids, they have a hotel now by Hilton, the Ferris wheel is reasonable but they Kids Museum should be $15 for each kid not $21 I’ve been there it’s cute but not wroth $21 and you know the smaller kids will get bored/ be ready to go. Maybe let the kids test it out first then they pay…. They have passes they give you a discount membership are good if you actually like going place and the have an Opera House Im going to check out !!

We absolutely love Navy Pier and make it a must-do every time we stay in Chicago. It doesn’t matter what season we’re visiting—there’s always something going on. From seasonal events and attractions to shops you can wander through and plenty of places to stop for a snack or a drink, it’s always lively and entertaining without feeling overwhelming.

One of our favorite parts is simply the walk itself. Strolling along the pier with Lake Michigan stretching out beside you and the city skyline in the background is hard to beat. The views of Chicago are truly phenomenal and offer some of the best photo opportunities in the city, especially at sunrise or sunset.

Even if you don’t plan to shop, eat, or drink, Navy Pier is still absolutely worth the visit for the scenery alone. It’s a great place to slow down, take in the views, people-watch, and enjoy the energy of the city. Whether it’s your first time in Chicago or your tenth, Navy Pier never disappoints and always feels like a classic Chicago experience.

Such a great place to visit both as a visitor and resident.  Open space, cleaner air, generally safe during the day and plenty to do.  There is some really neat boats and attractions unique to the area.  Great for walking around and getting to other spot attractions.  Again go during the summer or busy time with tourists, generally feels safer and more police presence.

I had been there during the summer days and it was absolutely a memorable experience- lots of people, nice weather, walk along the river, food/drink all nice. And then I visited the same place in winter (last week of Jan) and it was so difficult to even stay out or stay still outside for 5 minutes. Shops are closed, almost no one in the streets. A very different place..,.
I had a wonderful experience at the Chicago Riverwalk! It's such a lovely spot right by the river, perfect for a peaceful stroll. The massive buildings create a stunning backdrop.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Shoreline Sightseeing Architecture River Tour – Chicago, IL

Disclaimer: This is my personal experience. If something doesn’t go quite right, I always recommend giving the team a chance to make it right. Most places truly care. That said, this tour was everything I hoped for.

This was hands-down one of the best ways to experience downtown Chicago. The views from the river are incredible, and the guide gave a perfect mix of storytelling, architecture, and local history. I learned more in 75 minutes on the water than I expected.

The boat was comfortable, clean, and had plenty of space to sit and take in the sights. We cruised all three branches of the river and saw Chicago from angles you just can't appreciate from street level.

If you’re visiting the city,  or even if you live nearby,  I highly recommend it. It’s a relaxing, informative, and beautiful way to see Chicago’s skyline.

Pro tip: Sit on the upper deck and bring a light jacket. The wind off the river can surprise you.

This city is very amazing! I don't normally like big cities, but I have really liked and enjoyed Chicago. If I were to move to the big city, I'd like to go to Chicago for sure. The Riverwalk was very cool to pass through and the scenery was very nice -- it was just so cold to be around there at that time!

🌊 My Visit to Chicago Riverwalk

The Chicago Riverwalk is a beautiful and vibrant spot in downtown Chicago, offering a perfect blend of scenic views, dining, and entertainment along the Chicago River. Walking along the promenade, I enjoyed the stunning views of the city’s iconic skyscrapers and unique architectural bridges.

The Riverwalk is lined with cafés, bars, and restaurants, making it a great place to relax, grab a coffee, or enjoy a meal while watching boats glide by. I also loved the public art installations and small green spaces along the way, adding charm to the urban environment.

For those interested in more active experiences, you can rent kayaks or take boat tours that provide a new perspective of Chicago’s architecture and history. The atmosphere is lively yet relaxed, making it a wonderful place for both daytime strolls and evening outings.

📍 Tips for Visitors:
•	Best to visit during daylight for sightseeing or evening for beautiful city lights.
•	Wear comfortable shoes — there’s plenty to walk and explore.
•	Combine with a visit to Millennium Park or a boat tour of the Chicago River.

Overall, the Chicago Riverwalk is a must-visit for anyone wanting to experience the city’s charm, architecture, and waterfront vibes. 🚤🌆

A wonderful experience... we went during the off season in early February.  We got right in and we were given extra time on the Skydeck platform (because noone was waitin).  We timed it to watch a sunset from the 103rd floor and Mother Nature didn't disappoint. The elevator up was fast & very smooth.  Amazing views even in winter!!

Super cool. Its definitely a must visit if you've never been to the city. Awesome views and perspective. I recommend going during the weekday. We didn't have to wait at all and we had plenty of time to read all the information at the displays. Which is fun and educational.
Also, once at the top we were able to enjoy our time taking pictures and not having to rush. Ive been told some people wait hours, or it takes hours. Our visit from start to finish was about 45min. Got to see everything and anything we wanted.
Once your finished there's great restaurants on the main floor, various types of food and desserts.

I came here at the request of a family member who was visiting Chicago, and on a winter day with temps in the negatives, who was I to decline the suggestion? Tickets are easy to get in the off season and there was no wait to get to the observation deck. There are many photo opportunities on the way up, which I’m sure creates a line in the summer months.
The space itself is quite beautiful, providing 360 degree views of the city and lakefront.
The Tilt feature is a bit silly, I’m sure kids would have a good time with it, but as an adult, it was a novelty at most.
There are many places to get photos and for your photos to be taken.
The CloudBar was very fun! There are many themed drinks available, and the staff were helpful and friendly.
Overall it was a great experience, although definitely a tourist attraction.

We visited the Skydeck totally spontaneously on a Saturday night and had such a great experience! We were originally going to buy tickets at the self-checkout kiosk, but the prices were pretty high, so I checked online and found a much better deal through Tripster. The tickets were emailed instantly and we walked right in with the barcodes.

Before reaching the ledge, you go through a really well-done museum with interactive displays about Chicago’s history, architecture, and the Willis Tower. It definitely adds to the whole experience.

Once you get to the Skydeck, the line looks long but moves quickly and efficiently. We actually got to go out on the ledge multiple times and take plenty of photos. They take one professional photo from above, then you get 90 seconds to take your own. At the end, all the photos taken of you get uploaded to a card they give you, and you can download a few digital postcards for free.

Even though it was raining the night we went, the views were still breathtaking. It’s a little pricey, but honestly worth it — it’s not something you do every day, and the views alone make it memorable. We’d love to come back on a clear day to get more pictures together.

Highly recommend for both locals and visitors!

Very unique experience. There’s a small walk tour of the history of Chicago. You take an elevator to the top and then there’s diff sections for you to take pics. If you go during non busy hours they’ll let you stay in your box taking pics longer than if it was busy. We went at night and the views were still so pretty

Had the chance to check out 360 Chicago, and it was a great experience. The views were beautiful and definitely worth seeing — you get an amazing perspective of the city and the lake.

The space itself was very nice, clean, and well maintained. It also wasn’t too crowded, probably because I went on a weekday, which made the experience even more enjoyable and relaxing.

Overall, it was a lovely visit and a great spot to take in the Chicago skyline.

This observation deck is located on the 94th floor of the historic John Hancock Center, right on the Magnificent Mile in the heart of downtown Chicago. On a clear day, it offers a 360-degree panoramic view of the legendary skyline of the "Windy City" and beyond. Since we were here on a weekday, we didn't have to wait at the entrance or the elevator. Once at the top, we were treated to a breathtaking view of Michigan Avenue, Lake Michigan, and the classic Chicago landmarks with their authentic architecture. If you like, you can enjoy coffee, ice cream, snacks, and drinks at the Architect's Corner Café & Bar while taking in the stunning view. At $35 per person, the tickets weren't exactly cheap, but they were worth it for the experience. If you want to see Chicago from above, this is the place to go!

The observation deck followed by a sit down meal was the perfect way to end our last night in Chicago! The romantic supper was an excellent way to enjoy the view, watch the sunset and day become night. The staff made the experience even more special by making dessert for our anniversary! Just wonderful! Highly recommend!!!

This was my first time visiting this location, and I opted for this over the Sky Deck after reading numerous reviews praising its superior view compared to the Willis Tower. The check-in process was smooth, and as person who visited NYC several times, I found the experience to be less overwhelming comparing to NYC sky decks. I had high hopes for the elevators, expecting some impressive visuals, but unfortunately, there were none.

The view from the top was indeed breathtaking, but I must point out that the windows were not well-maintained. This made it difficult to appreciate the view properly and posed a challenge for taking good pictures.

I also tried the Tilt, which was a pleasant experience. It wasn’t as scary as I had anticipated, considering I’m not acrophobic. The Tilt was the only place where the windows were clear enough to view the cityscape effectively.

Apart from the Tilt, there’s a bar area, but as I mentioned earlier, the point of having a bar if the windows are not clean is lost.

Overall, it was a good experience and a worthwhile tourist attraction.

Chicago 360 is one of those places that actually lives up to the hype. The moment you step onto the observation deck, the view just hits you. You can see the entire city stretched out below, Lake Michigan looking endless, and on a clear day, it honestly feels like Chicago has no boundaries. Being almost 1,000 feet above the ground makes everything look calm and unreal at the same time.

The highlight for most people is TILT, and yes, it’s worth trying at least once. Slowly leaning out over Michigan Avenue is scary for a second, but also incredibly fun. It feels safe, well-managed, and adds a unique experience that you won’t find at most observation decks. Even if you skip TILT, the floor-to-ceiling glass windows give you amazing views and perfect angles for photos and videos.

What I liked most is how relaxed the space feels. You’re not rushed, there’s room to sit, take it all in, and actually enjoy the moment. Sunset is the best time to visit because you get daylight views, golden skies, and then the city lighting up at night—all in one visit.

There’s also a small café and bar, which makes the experience even better. Overall, Chicago 360 is not just about views; it’s about slowing down, appreciating the city from above, and leaving with memories that genuinely feel special.

Was there on january during the coldest month in chicago, as asian i was freezing 🤣
However the place was clean and very nice for a sight seeing
And definately the beans! The must have venue to take pict at
And we experience chicago hospitality during our walk there… amazing experience

A Chicago Must-Visit: Iconic Art and Family Fun!
​Millennium Park is easily the highlight of downtown Chicago. You cannot visit the city without stopping by "The Bean" (Cloud Gate). It is absolutely wonderful—photos don't do justice to how cool the reflections of the skyline look on its surface. It’s a great spot for family photos and just mesmerizing to walk under.
​The park is also incredibly kid-friendly, especially the skating area. We spent a lot of time at the McCormick Tribune Ice Rink (and the nearby Maggie Daley Skating Ribbon). The kids absolutely loved the skating experience; it’s well-maintained and provides a magical atmosphere with the city skyscrapers towering all around you.
​Whether you're there for the art, the skating, or just to let the kids run around the nearby play gardens, it’s a perfect (and mostly free!) day out. Highly recommend it for families!

Millennium Park is an absolute must! The design is beautiful not to mention The famous Bean installation is fab and not as overrun with people as you might imagine. Note: if you are sensory sensitive or otherwise inpaired the mirrored surface (especially the underbelly which can be a little intense) provides a distorted reality that might tie your brain in knots. We loved how when you walk around and underneath it, the reflection of the buildings and  skyline shift with you! Clever and interactive stuff!

Kinda a must see if in Chicago and if you catch it in early morning you could definitely get some great shots of the Bean and the City Scapes it provides.
Very popular attraction with large crowds coming and going all day and into the evening. We had limited time while in town and was able to walk from our hotel to visit. Spent about and hour getting photos and watching the crowds.

Millennium Park is one of the most famous parks around the world. Finally I saw the Bean which I have seen many times in movies. Surprisingly it is smaller than what I imagined. There are a lot of great spots to take pictures in or near this square. Here is Chicago.

Just a cool city spot. Like it's really nothing but it's cool. This is a big metal sculpture shaped in a bean that's a mirror and you can take pictures with it using the mirror or as a background. And you get great views of the Chicago skyline.  It's a nice calm park in the middle of the hustle of the city. I love this spot!

Cloud Gate is one of those landmarks that actually lives up to the hype. Sitting in the heart of Millennium Park, the massive mirrored sculpture reflects the Chicago skyline in a way that’s both surreal and iconic. No matter how many photos you’ve seen, standing in front of it—and especially walking underneath it—feels different in person.

The design is simple but genius. The reflections bend and stretch the city, the sky, and everyone around you, turning a quick stop into something oddly interactive. It’s free, easy to access, and surrounded by some of the best views in downtown Chicago, making it a must-see whether it’s your first visit or your fifth.

It can get crowded, especially during peak tourist hours, but early mornings or evenings offer a calmer experience and better photos. In any season—sunny, snowy, or overcast—Cloud Gate delivers a uniquely Chicago moment.

Visiting Cloud Gate in Chicago was truly an amazing experience. The moment we arrived, we were greeted by perfect weather that made the entire visit even more enjoyable. The sculpture itself is stunning—its mirror-like surface reflecting the skyline, the people, and the energy of the city in such a unique and captivating way.

What made it even more magical was seeing it at night. With the holiday lights glowing all around Millennium Park, the whole area felt festive and enchanting. The lights danced beautifully across the reflective surface of the Bean, creating picture-perfect moments from every angle.

Overall, it was an unforgettable visit—beautiful, vibrant, and absolutely worth experiencing, especially during the holiday season.

I visited Cloud Gate on Thanksgiving Day, and even with the heavy crowd, the energy felt electric. The Bean was packed with visitors, but I still managed to grab some great photos and enjoy the reflections. Walking through Millennium Park afterward rounded out the experience—lively, scenic, and worth every minute. It’s one of those Chicago stops that delivers, no matter how busy it gets.

The Bean as it's locally known as is a cool sculpture in a nice park. Definitely one of the more unique things in town.

As others have mentioned, I'd advise going early or at least before shops open as it'll be quieter. We also noticed it was quieter when it snowed so keep that in mind if you want to explore Chicago in the snow

Grant Park is truly the heart of downtown Chicago — a huge, green space surrounded by some of the city’s most iconic landmarks. It’s the perfect place to take a peaceful walk, enjoy the skyline, or simply sit under a tree and watch people go by. The Buckingham Fountain is stunning, especially when it’s lit up at night or during one of the hourly water shows. The park is always clean and well-kept, with plenty of open lawns and shaded paths for jogging or picnicking. It’s also home to major events like Lollapalooza, so depending on the season, you might catch live music or cultural festivals happening here. What I like most is how easy it is to reach from anywhere downtown — just a short walk from Millennium Park or the Art Institute. The combination of city energy and natural beauty makes Grant Park an ideal spot for both locals and tourists. Whether you’re looking for a morning run, an afternoon picnic, or an evening stroll, you’ll always find something enjoyable here.

One of my favorite urban parks I’ve ever had the pleasure of frolicking through. Expansive views of the Chicago skyline, Lake Michigan, extensive ornamental plantings, Buckingham Fountain, and more than enough spots to find a Chicago hot dog. Even for a quick 30 minute walk, it’s a wonderful place in the city to find some grass to touch.

Grant Park is a great spot to relax, walk, or enjoy the skyline views. It’s clean, spacious, and well-maintained, with easy access to nearby attractions like Millennium Park, Buckingham Fountain, and the Art Institute. Whether you're taking a stroll or just sitting on a bench, it’s a peaceful break from the busy downtown area.

This park is perfect for festivals and events.  In fact, the day we visited, there was a large food festival going on.  There were many vendors and it was well organized.  Security was on hand and there were plenty of washrooms.  The giant "Bean" attracted alot of attention.

This park is wonderful—clean, well-maintained, and full of charm! Whether you visit during the day or at night, you’ll get stunning views of the Chicago skyline and Lake Michigan. It’s a beautiful green space right in the heart of the city and the South Loop, making it perfect for both tourists and locals. You can enjoy peaceful garden walks, relax in nature, and take in some of the best urban scenery Chicago has to offer.

Willis Tower is an absolute must if you want to see Chicago from a whole different level—literally. The Skydeck on the 103rd floor delivers jaw-dropping views that stretch for miles on a clear day, giving you a true sense of how massive and flat the Midwest really is.

The highlight is the glass ledges that extend out from the building, letting you stand over the city with nothing but glass beneath your feet. It’s equal parts thrilling and nerve-wracking, even if you’re not afraid of heights. The experience is well-organized, informative, and worth the wait.

While tickets aren’t cheap and lines can get long, especially during peak hours, the payoff is undeniable. Sunset is the best time to visit if you want to see the city transition from daylight to a sea of lights.

The views from the top of Willis Tower are beautiful and absolutely worth it. I explored Chicago during a really long layover, and deciding to go up here was one of the best choices I made.

Chicago is a stunning city, and seeing it from above really puts its architecture and layout into perspective. When I visited, I honestly thought it was the most beautiful city I had ever seen that is, until I flew into Singapore the next day.

If you have time to explore Chicago, even during a layover, going to the top of Willis Tower is a must.

The best city views! Born and raised in Chicago, I’ve been coming here since I was a kiddo and it was Sears tower. Since then I come here with family or friends out of town, and this past trip back home to Chicago, I got to do yoga on the 99th floor and visit the skydeck. It was amazing :)

The Willis Tower (Sears Tower) is one of those places that actually lives up to the hype.
The views from the Skydeck are unreal and give you a true sense of how big and beautiful Chicago is. Stepping onto the glass ledge is a little nerve-racking at first, but it ends up being a really fun and memorable experience. The Willis Tower (Sears Tower) is one of those places that actually lives up to the hype. Everything felt well run and easy, and the staff were friendly and helpful throughout.
On top of that, there's just something special about being in such an iconic building. If you're in Chicago, this is absolutely worth the visit.

Bought tickets online the night before to save time.  Zero visibility on the day I had tickets for. Isn't that how it always works. Lol.  I haven't been there since I was a child, when it was still owned by Sears.  Now there's gift shops, donut shops, photo ops, and all kinds of other stuff! There was a color workshop for kids (maybe for adults too I'm not sure) before going in.  18 mph is how fast the trip to the 103rd floor is. VERY FAST! Oh my geez. Even with zero visibility it was good fun, especially for my teens that have never experienced a lot of the things I got to do in the city when I was a kid.

Beautiful and well thought attraction just steps away from cloud gate aka the bean at Millennium park.
The fountain is dual-purpose the actual fountain where you can see kids and adults enjoy the fountain on a hot summer afternoon by soaking themselves.  This is an interesting sight to see and never would have expected to see this. It was fun to watch.
The next thing is that these tall towers of fountains have displays that feature expressions of faces and beautifully displayed. It's amazing to see that and fun to watch. This is a must-see, and make sure to stay away from the people soaking in the fountain as they could get you soaked as well !!

Crown Fountain is one of the most creative and interactive attractions in Millennium Park, and it’s a must-see when visiting downtown Chicago. The fountain features two tall glass towers facing each other, displaying digital videos of Chicago residents’ faces. Every few minutes, water flows gently down the sides — and then, to everyone’s delight, sprays from the “mouths” of the faces in the video! It’s such a unique blend of art and fun, especially for kids who love splashing around on hot summer days. The reflection of the towers in the shallow water creates a beautiful effect, perfect for photography and relaxing by the edge. The area is clean, safe, and surrounded by trees and benches, making it a great place to cool off or just sit and enjoy the atmosphere. At night, the changing lights and videos make it even more magical. It’s also right next to Cloud Gate, so you can easily visit both in one stop. Whether you come to take pictures, play in the water, or admire the digital art, Crown Fountain captures Chicago’s playful and artistic spirit perfectly.

A wonderful spot for families and pet owners! The area is spacious, lively, and free to enter. Even with some buildings under renovation, it’s still a fun and relaxing place to walk around, take photos, and enjoy time together. Definitely pet-friendly and family-friendly!

A unique and mesmerizing installation in Millennium Park that perfectly blends art and play. The two glass towers displaying digital faces are fascinating to watch and the water feature brings a joyful and refreshing energy especially on warm days. It’s a place where locals and visitors gather relax and take photos while enjoying the lively atmosphere of the park. Simple creative and full of character it captures the fun and artistic spirit of Chicago beautifully.

**Crown Fountain**
A must-see attraction in Millennium Park! Work harder, going about getting down there, real soon, check it out!
**Key Features:**
- Iconic landmark
- Beautiful architecture
- Fun water features

What a fun experience. Our family went for the first time and we interacted with most exhibits multiple times. We were lucky that it wasn't too busy in the beginning so there were staff members who were able to help us take photos. I do love there are indicators for places you get the best photos for certain exhibits. I'm pretty sure my kids went through the vortex like 20 times. We really enjoyed our experience and have already told others about the fun.

My husband and I went for the first time on Valentines Day. It was pretty crowded so we didn't take too much time at each exhibit. We plan to return on a weekday to really immerse ourselves in all it has to offer. Got really great photos though.

We visited the Museum of Illusions on a random Wednesday for a day date, and we had so much fun! The exhibits were amazing and super interesting, interactive, and just overall a great time. Our absolute favorites were the infinity mirrors and the room where you look giant in one corner and tiny in another. We were cracking up the whole time!

Cole was incredibly gracious and took our pictures in the optical illusion chair and the giant/small room, he made sure we got the perfect shots. Adriana at the front desk was so helpful and walked us through getting a discount for booking online. Oscar guided us through the exhibits and made sure we understood how everything worked, which we really appreciated.

And shoutout to Louie in the puzzle room, we spent so much time in there completely stuck and mesmerized by the escape-style game! The entire staff was friendly, from helping with my coat to making sure my phone was safe. Everyone was so welcoming and really made the experience even better.

POV: You actually planned a fun Valentine’s date 😍
Went to the Museum of Illusions Chicago and it was SO worth it. Every room is interactive, trippy, and perfect for cute couple pics. We were laughing the whole time and got the best TikTok content 📸✨
Way better than just dinner. 10/10 date idea in Chicago 💘

We visited the Museum of Illusions Chicago and there was no wait time, which was nice. The front desk staff were very friendly and the check-in process was quick and smooth.

The museum itself was okay, but we expected more. The number of exhibits is quite limited, and for the price, it doesn’t really feel worth it. One hour is more than enough to see everything. Some illusions were interesting and fun, while others were not that impressive.

Overall, we enjoyed a few parts, but not enough to justify the regular ticket price, so I’d give it three stars. I’d recommend visiting only if you’re using a City Pass or getting a discounted ticket. At a lower price, it makes more sense.

This is one of the best free things to do in Chicago if you’re visiting. I was amazed by the grand architecture and the mosaic details. It has number of temporary exhibitions, room to wander, and beautiful interiors. I believe there’s 3 or 4 floors and you can access all floors using an elevator or if it’s not too much trouble take the stairs which is not a far walk. There’s also a gift shop filled with Chicago creators’ art which I recommend checking out.

Very cool space just across from Cloud Gate to wander around for free. There are a couple free exhibits and larger spaces for events. If you do find the glass domes they are really spectacular. Navigating can be a bit tricky so either look online for a map or take a picture of one posted by the main entrances.

By chance enter this place when I was heading to the Bean. Amazing interior design with historical decos. As I have limited time and rush to the other places so there’s not really enough time to enjoy all the art pieces and the stories behind. Make sure you can reserve at least an hour inside if you are into modern art ;)

One of Chicago's best free and must visit places. Historical building that hosts artists and exhibitions. We attended the SHIFT architecture biennial this month. The exhibit spanned multiple floors and was thoughtfully curated.

I absolutely love the Chicago Cultural Center!! Definitely a must visit for anyone traveling to Chicago, especially those looking for something interesting to do on a budget because it is always free to the public! There were two exhibits when I visited, both of which were incredible! The building itself is also just very beautiful so even if you don't have time to explore the exhibits, I would recommend just walking around inside to see it.
Summer workout series is my favorite event held on the great lawn.
Woah! This place was awesome ! I would love to catch a decent concert here. I can only imagine how crazy this venue would be for a metal show. Man that would be sick!

OMG, I am so lucky to witness the rehearsal of the most talented musicians all over the world in the park.
Double Base Xavier Foley was there, did anyone realize he didn't even have to read sheet music. Every note is in his mind.
And the conductor Carlos Kalmar, awesome conductor.
As a resident from San Francisco I amso envy of Chicago Residents, your city treats you so nice.

Love musical events held on the Great lawn. The sounds system is great. They allow outside food and drink. Amazing for a family picnic, date night, or girls night. Facility is clean and well taken care off.
This place is awesome! You can enjoy concerts with great views
The bridge is absolutely beautiful! We weren’t able to do any of the cruises due to the weather and parts of the river being iced over, but the architecture was great to admire.

Great views from here for sightseeing, and great for pictures. Great within walking distance and spacious despite packed Saturday afternoon. Variety of buildings, dining, and tourist options nearby. Great connection to Magnificent Mile.

The DuSable Bridge is one of Chicago’s true gems, offering some of the best views of both the skyline and the river. As you walk across, you’re treated to a perfect blend of history, architecture, and vibrant city life. It’s more than just a bridge—it’s a landmark that gracefully connects the Magnificent Mile to the bustling Loop, making it a vital link in the heart of the city. The elegant design reflects Chicago’s rich architectural heritage, while the sweeping views of the river and surrounding buildings create a breathtaking backdrop, especially at sunset. Standing on the bridge, you can really feel the pulse of downtown Chicago, making it an absolute must-see for anyone exploring the city.
10/10 experience. I was walking to go get pizza and stumbled across this bridge and this beautiful view. If you’re in Chicago, definitely check it out on a summer night.

DuSable Bridge is one of the best spots to stop and take in the city. The views are amazing; especially on a sunny day when the river turns this bright greenish-blue color. You can watch boats pass under the bridge, from little kayaks to big architecture tour boats.
Right around the bridge, there’s some of Chicago’s most famous buildings. The Wrigley Building looks super classic with its white exterior and big clock, and across from it is the Tribune Tower with its unique old-school design. If you look down the river, you’ll also see the shiny Trump Tower.
It’s a great place to pause, take some photos, and just enjoy the view. Easy to get to and worth a quick stop if you’re walking along Michigan Avenue or the Riverwalk.

If you’re in Chicago, the guided tour is an absolute "bucket list" experience—even if it’s not game day.

The Highlights:
• The History: Our guide was a walking encyclopedia of Cubs lore. Learning about the "Friendly Confines," the legends who played here (like Ernie Banks and Ron Santo), and the stadium's unique architecture was fascinating.

• Exclusive Access: We got to step into the Cubs’ dugout, walk along the warning track, and see the famous ivy-covered walls up close. Sitting in the press box offered a perspective of the field you just can't get from the bleachers.

• The Clubhouses: Since it was a non-game day, we were able to tour the visitor’s clubhouse. Standing in the same room where icons like Babe Ruth and Roberto Clemente once sat was a surreal moment for any sports fan.

• The Atmosphere: Even without 40,000 fans in the stands, you can feel the energy of the park. The tour is well-paced (about 75–90 minutes) and offers incredible photo opportunities at every turn.

My Pro Tip:
If you have the choice, book a non-game day tour. You get much more access to the "restricted" areas like the clubhouses and the dugout. Also, wear comfortable shoes—you'll be doing a fair amount of walking through history!

Wrigley Field isn't just a stadium; it’s a living museum. Whether you’re a die-hard Cubs fan or just a traveler who loves iconic landmarks, this tour is worth every penny.

Coming from a lifelong Cubs fan, Wrigley did not disappoint. Even though it wasn't open, I was so excited to see everything around it.  The neighborhood is cozy and safe, even after dark.  I hope to get back for a game someday!

One of the greatest ballparks on Earth. Period.

Wrigley is one of those stadiums that just hits different. It’s right up there with Tiger Stadium and Fenway for me — that perfect mix of history, charm, and “yeah, this place has seen some things.”

We’ve been here for games and the stadium tour, and honestly, the tour is a must-do. You get into the dugout, walk the warning track, climb into the bleachers, and see the ivy up close. The grounds crew even trimmed a little piece of ivy for my son — which immediately became the most valuable item in our house.

Game days are classic Wrigley: loud, fun, and occasionally soaked in rain because, well… Midwest weather does whatever it wants. Even then, it’s still iconic.

Wrigleyville itself is awesome — bars, food, energy, chaos, the whole deal. There’s nothing like walking out of the park straight into that neighborhood vibe.

If you love baseball at all, or even just appreciate old-school venues that haven’t been copy-pasted from modern stadium blueprints, Wrigley is absolutely worth the trip.

4 stars for the history of this ballpark.
You can't go there expecting the amenities of modern stadiums.
You have to embrace the history ask simplicity of this place.
The fan experience outside on wrigleyville is one of the best that I have seen at an MLB ballpark.

Beautiful historic place. Game was fantastic. Everybody was really nice. Stadium was spotlessly clean lines were big, but I thought they were handled well. Easy to find a bathroom that didn’t have a lineup all very clean as well and air conditioned. Would be happy to see a game there again..

Loved it! I was there during thanksgiving break at it wasn’t busy at all giving me time to browse mostly everything. I bought the ticket online literally 30 min before going in, free maps at the entrance will help you navigate the building without the needs of a guide. The gift shop is also great 👍🏼

I came in with no expectations and I left feeling a little disappointed.

While I appreciate the desire to dedicate a museum to a tradition of American writing, the vast majority of writers they cited were white and all writers were exclusively from the continental United States. There was no acknowledgement of other languages in American writing, nor a single indigenous American author.

I want to believe that this is just the start and that the institution will grow to truly encompass what its namesake suggests.

In my role as an English teacher, I had the opportunity to visit the museum for a field trip with two colleagues and 47 ninth graders from our school in Villa Park, Illinois. Students expressed lots of excitement, joy, inspiration, wonder, respect, curiosity and interest during and after our visit, and it was common to hear that they wished it had lasted longer. Some expressed interest in returning.

The manageable size of the museum, its focus on literary history, diverse genres, and individual authors, and the stylish visual design and interactivity of the exhibits made for an aesthetically and intellectually satisfying, as well as fun, field trip. The typewriters were the most popular feature, and students expressed their appreciation for the knowledgeable and kind staff members who guided and assisted them.

Great little museum for anyone interested in American literature, American history, or both!

Very interactive exhibits with something for everyone.

I had a wonderful experience there I really got to know new things about American history …there was this section where you can type your own story on the vintage typewriter that was very interesting …another part that I liked was there were some cards hanging where immigrants can share there own story in their own words …those little notes were so impactful and really tells a lot about immigrants’ struggle … the Museum has also a section where you can read the books of renowned writers and learn about American culture and history.
I definitely recommend visiting this museum
Painted by Dwight White II in October 2020 to recognize downtown workers. Sponsored by the Chicago Loop Alliance.

A walking trip through downtown Chicago is an experience full of energy, flavor, and unforgettable city views. The moment you step into the heart of the city, you’re surrounded by towering skyscrapers, historic buildings, and the constant buzz of urban life. The architecture alone makes the walk worthwhile, with modern glass towers standing proudly beside classic stone landmarks.

Strolling along the Chicago Riverwalk is one of the best ways to begin. The calm water reflects the skyline beautifully, and you can see boats cruising by while people relax along the waterfront. As you continue walking, you’ll find countless restaurants, cafés, and bars offering everything from deep-dish pizza to gourmet street food. Grabbing a slice of Chicago-style pizza or a hot dog loaded with toppings feels like a must. Pair it with a locally brewed craft beer, and you truly get a taste of the city’s character.

Downtown also offers peaceful green spaces like Millennium Park, where you can relax between sightseeing stops. The park’s open lawns, art installations, and street performers create a lively yet laid-back atmosphere. As evening approaches, the city transforms. Streetlights glow, buildings light up, and the skyline looks even more magical.

Walking through the Magnificent Mile adds a touch of excitement with its luxury shops, vibrant sidewalks, and festive energy. Every corner of downtown Chicago feels alive — from casual beer spots to elegant rooftop bars. Whether you’re exploring with friends, family, or on your own, downtown Chicago offers a perfect mix of food, fun, parks, and stunning urban beauty that leaves a lasting impression.

Very nice place full of life thousands of activities are nice I like it too much from all of the US countries. It’s very special experience. People are friendly whether it is nice too much activities. Going on different culture restaurants I recommend recommended to everybody.

Downtown Chicago has an energy that feels nonstop. Between the world-class museums, striking architecture, historic buildings, the L train looping overhead, endless shopping, and iconic spots like the Bean and the fountains, there’s always something to see or do. Overall, downtown Chicago gave me an incredible mix of culture, history, and pure city buzz—I loved every minute of it.

Minding my business driving out of my parking lot I stop to take pics of this view😅

Well here’s the serious review:
Downtown Chicago is a dynamic blend of old-world charm and modern sophistication, where towering glass skyscrapers rise alongside historic Art Deco buildings. Whether you're a tourist or a lifelong Chicagoan, The Loop the heart of downtown never ceases to amaze.

What stands out first is the architecture. From the iconic Willis Tower (formerly Sears Tower) to the ornate Chicago Theatre and the Tribune Tower, every street seems like a living museum. Take an architectural boat tour on the Chicago River it’s hands-down one of the best ways to understand the city's unique style and rich history.

The Millennium Park area is a perfect starting point, with the reflective "Cloud Gate" (The Bean), sprawling gardens, and frequent cultural events. Nearby, you’ll find the Art Institute of Chicago a world-class museum with everything from Monet to Warhol.

Food options are endless: deep-dish pizza from Lou Malnati's or Giordano’s, upscale steakhouses, food trucks near the park, and buzzing international restaurants. You’ll also find cozy cafes tucked between high-rises, perfect for a quiet break amid the bustle.

Public transport is easily accessible via the “L” trains, and most attractions are walkable. While it can be crowded during rush hours and busy seasons, it never feels unmanageable.

Overall, downtown Chicago has it all culture, food, shopping, entertainment, and a skyline that leaves a lasting impression. It’s a place where the city's gritty soul meets artistic elegance. A must-visit for anyone who loves cities with character.

Its a convenient way to navigate the city during harsh winters,rainy days and hot weather!the network of tunnels and bridges connecting downtown buildings make it easy to get around without braving the elements.

One of the perks that comes with living in San Diego is that you can enjoy great weather almost every day of the year.  Benefitting from this luxury creates a curiosity to experience the novelty of what others refer to as “cold weather”, so when I travel during the winter months I want to bundle up and then get outside!  🥶

I realize that living with freezing temperatures probably transitions from “novelty” to “torture” rather quickly, so I was curious about exploring a refuge for those less enthusiastic than myself about being out in the winter elements.

The Chicago Pedway System spans approximately 5 miles and connects 40 blocks as it weaves its way beneath the heart of the city.  In what feels like an underground city, this maze of tunnels features multiple CTA (”L”) and commuter rail stations and also provides access to 50 public and private buildings.  While there are shopping and food opportunities available, the pedway is more functional than a destination itself.

While the tunnels are well lit, clean, and safety never became a concern during my exploration, there are sections that can feel rather isolated (e.g., the parking garage segment), so the buddy system applies whenever possible.

The available routes are consistently marked by the pedway symbol (and several maps are posted), but you should be aware that the tunnels were not constructed in a contiguous fashion, so emerging from this subterranean sanctuary may be required at various intervals during your journey.

And that’s…Henry’s Take.

#chicagopedwaysystem

Great concept - not safe enough for our family.

We used the Chicago Pedway today for the first time to get from Millennium Park to Macy’s with our two young daughters. The idea behind the Pedway is great. Staying indoors during winter is a huge plus, especially with kids, and it was fairly easy to navigate.

We had a scary and completely unprovoked incident that made us decide we will not be using it again.

As we were approaching a doorway, a man (who we assume was on drugs or has mental health issues) came from the opposite direction. He walked through the doorway, past me and our 3-year-old, and went straight up to my husband, who was pushing our 1-year-old in the stroller. He immediately raised both fists and began swaying back and forth like he was about to punch my husband. He kept repeating, “That’s what I thought,” over and over, while blocking my husband from passing.

When I realized what was happening I headed for the baby, my husband did a quick circle to get her away from him and passed me the stroller as I came back toward them. I grabbed our daughters and kept walking. My husband calmly told the man “no,” repeatedly and eventually was able to  walk around him. Thankfully, the man didn’t follow.

What stood out was how isolated parts of the Pedway felt. It seemed like the kind of thing that wouldn’t have happened if more people had been around. The Pedway is a useful concept, but it needs a police presence or security.

It is a series of underground pathways for pedestrians. It links  large number of buildings, hotels, shopping and eating places. While most of the payday is clean and well kept There are places which are stinky and dirty. It is the best Refuge in times of rain and snow. I literally covered the whole of downtown Chicago using the pedway.
